High libertarian skepticism of state control (74.04) and very low agreeableness (0.61) drive opposition to regulation, though high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) and punitive attitudes (72.85) create some internal tension.

Extreme anti-authority libertarian views and disruptor personality oppose most regulation, but strong honesty-humility and fairness values create a slight pull toward accountability, resulting in mild opposition.

Strong authoritarianism (acceptance of coercive social control) and high conscientiousness support accountability, balanced by moderate anti-elitism and a competitive worldview skepticism of large-platform regulation.
Moderate support due to high trust in institutions but balanced by libertarian skepticism of regulation.

Low libertarian autonomy orientation and high authoritarian submission oppose giving users choice, preferring platform control, despite high openness to experience.

Low openness to experience and high authoritarian submission create mild opposition to giving users choice over algorithms, seen as potentially undermining social order and exposing people to harmful content, despite moderate digital literacy.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High fairness/reciprocity and trust in science support labelling to prevent deception, while libertarian concerns about over-regulation keep it at clear support.
Strong care/harm and fairness moral foundations support labelling to prevent deception, and high trust in science reinforces the need, though low digital literacy slightly dampens urgency.

High moral foundations for sanctity/purity (84.8%) and authority/subversion (99.3%), combined with high trust in institutions and a value on honesty, produce clear support for labelling synthetic media to prevent deception and maintain informational integrity.

High trust in science and experts, combined with high fact-checking behavior and a dangerous-worldview sensitivity, support mandatory labeling to prevent deception, while low authoritarianism and moderate libertarian anti-censorship views moderate the score.

Strong care/harm foundation (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) support labeling to prevent deception, reinforced by high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) and moderate trust in science (56.75).

High purity/sanctity concerns and disgust sensitivity actually support labeling deceptive media, but this is outweighed by extreme anti-regulatory and anti-censorship leanings, producing moderate opposition.

High conscientiousness and order values, plus moderate dangerous-worldview threat sensitivity, support labeling to prevent deception, but high global cosmopolitanism and low trust in institutional regulatory efficiency moderate the score.
High openness and care/harm foundation strongly support labeling to prevent harm, tempered by low authoritarianism.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high trust in government and institutions, combined with low system justification and high conscientiousness, produce strong opposition to election distrust narratives.
Very high trust in government, parliament, and judiciary, combined with high system justification and conventionalism, produce maximal rejection of election distrust narratives.

Very high trust in government (69.6%), parliament (80.3%), and police (92.4%), combined with high system justification (69%) and authoritarian submission (92%), produce maximal opposition to distrusting elections, as this would undermine the legitimate authority and social order she strongly supports.

Extremely low dangerous-worldview and competitive-jungle worldview, combined with high trust in the judiciary and police and moderate trust in government, produce very strong rejection of election distrust as paranoid and unfounded.

Despite low trust in government (34.96) and low external efficacy (37.35), very high system justification (72.26) and high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) produce clear opposition to election distrust, as it threatens institutional stability.

Extreme anti-elitism, Manichean worldview, very high collective narcissism, and prior justice system contact strongly support a narrative of government election distrust as validating systemic corruption.
Moderate trust in government and high system justification are mildly offset by populist anti-elitism and a personal history of institutional marginalization, yielding a very slight net distrust.
Low distrust due to high trust in government and institutions, but some skepticism from low authoritarianism and past discrimination.
